title: typeof null
description: A classic JavaScript quirk you'll encounter in interviews.
gotchaExplanation: typeof null returns "object" — this is a bug in JavaScript that has existed since the very first version and was never fixed to preserve backward compatibility.
patternExplanation: typeof returns a string describing the type. For null it incorrectly returns "object" instead of "null". Always use === null to check for null explicitly.

title: NaN === NaN
gotchaExplanation: NaN is the only JavaScript value that is not equal to itself. NaN === NaN is false by the IEEE 754 floating-point specification.
patternExplanation: Use Number.isNaN(value) to reliably check for NaN. The global isNaN() coerces the argument first, which can produce surprising results.

title: String + Number
gotchaExplanation: Operators evaluate left-to-right. 1 + '2' coerces 1 to a string, giving "12". Then "12" + 3 coerces 3 to a string, giving "123".
patternExplanation: When + has a string operand, all operands are coerced to strings. Evaluation is left-to-right, so the first + determines the chain.

title: var in a Loop
gotchaExplanation: var is function-scoped, so all three callbacks share the same i variable. By the time setTimeout fires, the loop has finished and i is 3.
patternExplanation: Replace var with let to create a new binding per iteration, printing 0, 1, 2. Alternatively wrap the callback in an IIFE to capture i by value.

title: Loose Equality Coercion
gotchaExplanation: [] is truthy, but == coercion converts both sides to numbers: [] becomes 0, false becomes 0. 0 == 0 is true.
patternExplanation: Loose equality (==) applies Abstract Equality Comparison: booleans convert to numbers, objects call valueOf/toString. Always use === to avoid these pitfalls.

title: Object Key Coercion
gotchaExplanation: Object keys are always strings. The array [1, 2] is coerced to "1,2" via .toString(), so obj[[1,2]] sets the key "1,2".
patternExplanation: When a non-string is used as an object key, JavaScript calls its .toString() method. Arrays join their elements with commas. Use Map if you need non-string keys.

title: Closure Counter
gotchaExplanation: Each call to counter() creates a new closure with its own count. a() is called three times (returning 1, 2, 3) and b() is called once. The fourth a() call returns 3.
patternExplanation: Each closure captures its own independent variable binding. a and b do not share state because each counter() call produces a fresh lexical environment.
